title_en: The Obligation of Acting on This Knowledge and the Path Forward
title_ar: وجوب العمل بهذا العلم والطريق إلى الأمام
content_en:
Ibn Abd al-Wahhab closes Al-Wajibat al-Mutahattamat by addressing the relationship between knowledge and action, and by laying out what the Muslim who has absorbed this foundational knowledge is now obligated to do.

The Sheikh draws on the famous hadith in which the Prophet, peace be upon him, described the person who acquires knowledge without acting on it as resembling a donkey carrying books — bearing the weight of knowledge without benefiting from it. Knowledge of Allah, of the Prophet, and of Islam carries with it an obligation to live by what one has learned. Iman that does not produce action is, in the Athari understanding, incomplete — the branches of faith (shu'ab al-iman) include both internal states and external practices.

The first obligatory action following from this knowledge is tawhid in practice: directing all acts of worship exclusively to Allah. The Muslim who has understood what shirk is and what tawhid requires must examine his own practices and those of his community. If he finds that people around him are calling upon the dead, seeking help from graves, or directing vows and sacrifices to other than Allah, it becomes his obligation to know the ruling on these practices and, to the extent of his ability and wisdom, to encourage correction.

The second obligation is following the Sunnah of the Prophet in all acts of worship. The knowledge that the religion is complete means that one is not free to innovate new forms of worship out of good intentions. Every act of worship must have a basis in the Quran or authentic Sunnah. The scholar Al-'Iz ibn Abd as-Salam stated: 'There is no worship except what Allah has legislated.' This principle must govern every aspect of the believer's religious practice.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab then addresses the obligation of seeking further knowledge beyond what this treatise covers. Al-Wajibat al-Mutahattamat is, by design, a brief primer — the minimum required of every Muslim. The seeker of knowledge must continue to study: to read the Quran with understanding, to learn the foundations of aqeedah in greater depth from the works of the scholars, to study the fiqh that governs daily worship and transactions, and to seek guidance from trustworthy scholars.

The Sheikh concludes with a reminder about the urgency of this knowledge. Death may come at any time, and the questions in the grave — 'Who is your Lord? What is your religion? Who is your prophet?' — require answers that flow from genuine knowledge and conviction, not rote repetition. The person who has truly internalized the obligations laid out in this treatise will be able to answer those questions with certainty, because his heart is settled on tawhid, his tongue testifies to the prophethood of Muhammad, and his practice follows the religion of Islam as the Prophet left it.

The treatise ends as it began: with the call to take seriously what Allah and His messenger have made obligatory. Every Muslim — scholar or layman, man or woman, old or young — shares in this obligation. There is no excuse for ignorance of these foundations when the scholars have made them so accessible.
